original_abstract: Neuroscience has historically served as a vital wellspring for breakthroughs in artificial intelligence. We argue that to drive the next wave of AI innovation, we must prioritize fundamental research in "NeuroAI." A cornerstone of this initiative is the embodied Turing test, which moves beyond traditional benchmarks like chess or language to challenge AI models to navigate the physical world with the agility and sensorimotor sophistication of living animals. By shifting our focus from uniquely human cognitive feats to the robust capabilities shared by all animals—skills honed over 500 million years of evolution—we can address the fragility of current AI systems. Developing models capable of passing the embodied Turing test will provide a powerful roadmap for creating the next generation of intelligent, flexible, and energy-efficient autonomous agents.
humanized_abstract: Neuroscience has always been a prime source of inspiration for work in AI. Now we believe it is time for a new field of fundamental neuroscience research for the development of NeuroAI. An essential aspect of this effort is what we call the “embodied Turing test” and an AI system that is able to successfully pass it. Instead of basing tests on capabilities unique to humans such as playing chess or speaking languages, in the embodied Turing test an AI system needs to interact effectively in the physical world much like a small animal and with the sensorimotor skills animals of all kinds have evolved over half a billion years of animal evolution. Thus instead of aiming for exceptionally human intelligence in an AI system as is the case with today’s weak AI and strong AI, the aim would be to reach a lower level of very general animal-like intelligence which can be sustained in embodied computational vehicles of a different design. The AI systems that could pass the embodied Turing test would be on a par with the basic animal ability to survive, and their design would serve as a guide for how to develop efficient, versatile and intelligent robots.
